TESTING BAITS

        The bait plasmid should be tested in yeast to show that it
directs the synthesis of the fusion protein, and that the bait enters
the nucleus, and binds LexA operators.

        To be useful in an interactor hunt, your bait must not
activate transcription by itself.  pSH18-34 (see REPORTERS) and yeast
strain EGY48 (see STRAINS) can be used to test whether your bait
activates transcription.  Introduce pSH18-34 and a bait plasmid into
EGY48 by transformation, and patch transformed colonies onto Xgal
indicator plates and leucine minus plates.  As described in the
accompanying handout from Steve Hanes (see REPORTERS), pSH18-34 is a
URA3, 2um plasmid containing the GAL1 promoter fused to lacZ, in which
the GAL1 enhancer-like Upstream Activating Sequence (UASG) has been
replaced with binding sites for 8 LexA dimers (4 colE1 operators, see
Golemis handout).  Baits that activate transcription cause expression
of beta-galactosidase from this reporter.  You should also ensure that
your bait does not activate the LexAop-LEU2 gene in EGY48 (see YEAST
STRAINS).  To do this, test whether your bait plasmid allow growth of
EGY48 in the absence of leucine.

        Baits that do not activate transcription should be tested to
see whether they enter the nucleus and bind LexA operators.  This can
be tested by using the bait plasmid with another reporter, pJK101, in
a repression or "blocking" assay (Brent and Ptashne, 1984, Nature,
312:612-615), which detects DNA binding by transcriptionally inert
LexA fusion proteins.  For this assay, EGY48 is cotransformed with the
bait plasmid and pJK101.  pJK101 is a URA3, 2um plasmid similar to
SH18-34, except that it contains most of the GAL1 upstream activating
sequence (UASG).  It also contains, in between UASG and the GAL1
transcription start, a colE1 derived LexA operator, which provides
high affinity binding sites for two LexA-bait protein dimers.  Yeast
harboring pJK101 will have significant beta-galactosidase activity
when grown on galactose medium.  Most lexA fusions that enter the
nucleus and bind the operators but do not activate transcription,
repress this beta-galactosidase activity from 2 to 20 fold.  We take
even 2 fold repression to indicate >50% operator occupancy by the
bait.

        Two HIS3+ control plasmids are commonly used for the
activation assay and repression assay.  One, pSH17-4 expresses a
LexA-GAL4 fusion that activates transcription.  The other, pRFHM1,
expresses LexA fused to a transcriptionally inert fragment of the
Drosophila Bicoid product (pEG202 does not serve as a good negative
control because the part encoded by the polylinker through to the
first stop codon activates transcription a bit).  pRFHM1 can also be
used as a control for the repression assay; the LexA-bicoid fusion
represses the activity of pJK101 by 2 to 5-fold.

Russ Finley
revised September 1993
